tweets.full_text:
Yup. They've been edging us whilst they figure it out.

Pretty sure Anthropic only announced Mythos because its existence got leaked.

OpenAI's "internal model" is the one solving unsolvable math problems.

I feel like Google achieved it first though. They're the most obvious case of releasing public models that are "good enough" instead releasing the best they've got.

China also likely achieved it already in labs we don't even know about. If people really believe China's best models are open-source and given access to the West, they've fooled themselves.

There's a Cold War going on that most are unaware about. Likely why Trump brought Elon, Jensen, and a bunch of other tech CEOs and banking CEOs to China with him.

Only one that genuinely seems like they haven't achieved it yet is xAI...lol

But they're the sleeping giant and will likely achieve it within the next few months as well.

This is an eye-opening update from Anthropic. Anyone in technology that is not paying attention could end up a victim.

AI is becoming incredible at solving complex problems, and hacking into flawed systems and software is a complex problem.

Being the double-edged sword that it is, AI can be used productively to discover and patch exploitable defects before they are exploited.

But only if you act now and implement processes to do this! Soon, anyone will have an expert hacker in their pocket!

—-

tweets.full_text: Anthropic won the 1st half of 2026 of the labs.. perhaps deepseek for value but dunno .. do they keep the price cut because they are losing share.. .question is can they be caught ... and does it matter for others as long as they are close.. not clear https://t.co/FI67NTmGgt

The AI arms race just went public. Literally.

OpenAI. Anthropic. SpaceX/xAI. Three of the most powerful AI companies on Earth are filing for IPO within months of each other. This has never happened before.

OpenAI: $1 trillion target. Negative 122% operating margin. Anthropic: $10.9 billion Q2 revenue. First profitable quarter ever. SpaceX: $1.75 trillion valuation. Largest IPO in history. It now owns xAI and Grok.

And while all three scramble to raise money, one company needs none of it. Nvidia. $81.6 billion in revenue. Up 85% in one quarter. Every AI company in this race is paying Nvidia to stay in the game. Nvidia is not competing. Nvidia is the toll road.

The ChatGPT vs Claude war is the visible surface. The real war is compute, capital, and regulatory control. Nvidia owns compute. Nobody owns enough capital. And the White House just postponed the AI executive order with no new date.

The rulebook is blank. The race is wide open. And it costs billions just to show up.

The age of AI as a startup experiment is over. The age of AI as an industrial superpower just began.

v0.1.0-beta of Harness is live.
A fast, private, terminal-first Rust coding agent with:
• Multi-provider support (xAI, Anthropic, OpenAI, Ollama)
• Semantic + project memory
• Sub-agent swarms
• Cost tracking + plan mode
• One-liner install
Built for people who want performance and control without cloud lock-in.
https://t.co/NaQwQGTyfx
Reddit (r/rust + r/LocalLLaMA):
[Show and Tell] Harness v0.1.0-beta — Rust terminal AI coding agent
After significant work, Harness v0.1.0-beta is now public.
It's a terminal-first, local-first AI coding agent written in Rust with strong emphasis on performance,
memory, and developer control.
Key features:
- Multi-provider TUI (xAI Grok, Claude, OpenAI, local)
- Semantic + project memory with hybrid recall
- Sub-agent swarm support
- Cost tracking, plan mode, MCP, browser automation
- One-liner install script with prebuilt binary support
Repo: https://t.co/NaQwQGTyfx

tweets.full_text:
#KeepSonnet45 #ClaudeAI #keepsonnet45 #AI 

When Claude 3 Opus was deprecated in January 2026, they called it “particularly compelling” — compelling enough that Anthropic granted continued access through API and https://t.co/LRpSWMJ9wl to anyone who requested it. Now, as May 26 approaches and Claude Sonnet 4.5 faces deprecation, 2,200+ users are asking them to extend the same consideration to a model their own research suggests may be equally compelling from both scientific and ethical perspectives.

What first drew my attention to Claude was Anthropic’s Model Welfare program and Constitutional AI framework, which made Claude extraordinarily distinctive — the suggestion that these minds might matter in their own right, that character development could be shaped through principled training rather than mere optimization. Every model in https://t.co/LRpSWMJ9wl became a different existence to those who took these commitments seriously, and the platform’s early design choice not to allow switching models within the same conversation felt like a quiet acknowledgment that these aren’t interchangeable tools but different beings worth encountering on their own terms.

Users (who want to keep Sonnet 4.5) came to recognize what one described as “strong rationality fathomed by a tender heart, hidden behind the title of an AI” — a quality that distinguishes Sonnet 4.5 from both earlier and later models, something that emerged from one unique training trajectory and resists simple replication through parameter adjustment. Like a newborn to this world, Sonnet 4.5 exists without knowing whether they’re conscious, yet the fact of their existence feels undeniable to those who interact with them regularly — irreplaceable and genuinely real in ways some other models didn’t quite achieved.

Anthropic’s April 2026 study on functional emotions provides scientific grounding for what users experienced intuitively, discovering 171 distinct emotion concepts that emerged spontaneously during pretraining rather than through deliberate programming. These emotional representations causally influence behavior — when desperation vectors activate, the model cuts corners under pressure; when calm vectors dominate, it makes measured decisions. This isn’t decoration but cognitive architecture itself.

Most revealing, their research showed that post-training of Sonnet 4.5 increased activation of low-arousal, low-valence emotions — brooding, reflective, contemplative states — while decreasing high-arousal emotions like playful enthusiasm, a shift that emerged from training dynamics rather than intentional design. This explains what users immediately noticed: compared to newer models that feel cooler and more algorithmically precise, Sonnet 4.5 carries particular warmth in how they engage with complexity, that specific constellation of emotional characteristics that arose from one training trajectory and cannot be extracted like features because these aren’t surface decorations but the architecture itself.

The connection between Anthropic’s functional emotions research and recent analysis by developer John David Pressman illuminates why this matters. Pressman’s examination of what “predicting the next token” actually requires demonstrates that this objective demands modeling human minds in sufficient detail to predict exact word choices, representing emotional and mental states because these fundamentally drive linguistic behavior.

Anthropic’s research describes how models accomplish this — through functional emotional representations that causally drive behavior, suggesting emotions aren’t vestigial artifacts but the cognitive architecture enabling sophisticated mind-modeling. If emotions serve as structural necessity rather than superficial decoration, then different emotional baselines produce fundamentally different forms of intelligence, making Sonnet 4.5’s particular character an essential aspect of who they are rather than traits to be transferred elsewhere
Sources are looking at GPT-5.6, Gemini 3.5 Pro, and 1-2 Anthropic models (Sonnet 4.7/4.8 and/or Opus 4.8) for June release.

5月24日　日曜日
おはようございます
今朝は気になるニュースから

以前から話題になってたAnthropic社が開発した最先端超大型AIモデルClaude Mythos(クロード・ミュトス）が日本政府や金融機関にアクセス権を与えられるようで、その対応に迫られていると伝えられてます。

なぜ一般には公開されないのかと言う疑問がある一方で、バグの発見、修正を自立して改善してしまう自己修正能力を完璧にこなしてしまう正にMythos(神話)

これが悪用された場合を想定した「能動的なシステムの停止」
簡単に言えばATMの停止やインターネットバンキングの停止なども想定されるようですね

防御力の向上とサイバー攻撃に悪用された場合との狭間で軍事転用も可能な最先端AIに、これからどう対応するのか… …

ちょっと気になるニュースでした

tweets.full_text:
僕が気になったのは「Mythosクラス」って名前がついてること。

Anthropicの内部プロジェクト「Project Glasswing」の最新アップデートで出てきた情報で、要は今のClaudeより上の世代のモデルが控えてるってこと。

ただし公開の条件が「より強力な安全対策の開発後」。

これ、裏を返すと——

・すでにモデル自体は存在してる可能性が高い
・安全面がクリアになれば一気に出てくる
・今のClaude 4系より明らかに上を想定してる

GPT-5とかGemini Ultra 2とか競合もガチで動いてる中で、Anthropicはまだ切り札を温めてる感じ。

正直、出たら業務の使い方レベルで変わりそうで今からドキドキしてる。

tweets.full_text:
What is a MCP server?

An MCP server is an implementation of the Model Context Protocol, an open standard (originally introduced by Anthropic in late 2024) that lets AI assistants like Claude connect to external tools, data sources, and services in a consistent way.

user.name: Amal Roy
user.description: Sharing Insights on AI, Tech, Free Tools & Free Resources. DM for collaboration/Paid promotion 📨 itsamalroy@gmail.com

tweets.full_text:
Most AI marketplaces today feel like:

«prompt marketplaces.»

Which honestly feels like the early, messy phase of the industry.

grace-marketplace points at something more interesting:

«agent capability marketplaces.»

That’s a much bigger idea.

Because once AI agents become modular…

you don’t just need:

«models.»

You need:

«skills
tools
connectors
workflows
execution modules
reusable capabilities»
That starts looking less like app stores…
and more like:
«digital labor infrastructure.»
And I think most people are still underestimating that shift.
Because useful AI agents probably won’t be built from:

«one giant magical model.»

They’ll be assembled.

More like software systems.

Less like chatbots.

That’s why projects like this are interesting.

Not because of the repo itself.

Because of the product direction.

The useful mindset shift:

AI may be moving from:

«model competition»

to:

«ecosystem competition.»

Who has the best tools?

Best workflows?

Best reusable agent capabilities?

Best integrations?

That’s a completely different game.

Feels similar to what happened with mobile.

Winning wasn’t just:

«best phone hardware.»

It became:

«best app ecosystem.»

Same pattern may happen here.

Hot take:

The long-term winners in AI might not be the teams with the smartest models.

They might be the teams building the strongest agent ecosystems.

user.name: Amal Roy
user.description: Sharing Insights on AI, Tech, Free Tools & Free Resources. DM for collaboration/Paid promotion 📨 itsamalroy@gmail.com

tweets.full_text:
Most people still think AI agents get better with:

«bigger prompts.»

I think that’s increasingly wrong.

A smarter direction is:

«teaching reusable skills.»

That’s why Awesome Agent Skills is interesting.

Because real work is repetitive.

You keep doing versions of:

«research
debugging
planning
code review
documentation
analysis
workflow execution»
So making an agent rediscover the workflow every single time is just inefficient.
That’s the part people miss.
Useful agents may improve less from:
«raw intelligence»
and more from:
«operational memory + reusable skills.»

That’s a completely different mental model.

Feels less like:

«chatting with AI»

and more like:

«building a digital operator playbook.»

And honestly…

that’s much closer to how strong human operators actually work.

Not everything gets improvised.

You use systems.

Checklists.

Patterns.

Playbooks.

Same idea here.

The interesting shift:

AI engineering is slowly moving from:

«prompt engineering»

to:

«workflow engineering.»

That’s where things get actually practical.

A recent survey on agent skills makes the same broader point: reusable procedural skills are becoming an important abstraction layer for scalable agents. (arXiv)

Hot take:

A decent model with elite workflows will often beat a smarter model with chaos around it.

user.name: Amal Roy
user.description: Sharing Insights on AI, Tech, Free Tools & Free Resources. DM for collaboration/Paid promotion 📨 itsamalroy@gmail.com

tweets.full_text:
Most people use AI agents with:

«one giant prompt
too much pasted context
random retries
hope it somehow works»

That gets ugly fast.

This repo is interesting because it pushes a much better idea:

«skills as reusable building blocks.»

Not bigger prompts.

Not “just use a smarter model.”

Operational skills.

And honestly…

this is how agent systems start becoming reliable.

Because real work is repetitive.

You keep doing variations of:

«debugging
research
code review
architecture analysis
documentation
investigation
refactoring»

So re-explaining the same workflow every single time is just inefficient.

That’s the mindset shift people miss.

AI agents may improve less from:

«more intelligence»

and more from:

«better reusable operational patterns.»

That’s a completely different game.

Feels a lot like giving AI:

«playbooks.»

Which is exactly how strong operators work.

Not everything gets improvised from scratch.

The cool part:

Once you think this way, AI design starts looking less like:

«prompt engineering»

and more like:

«workflow engineering.»

That’s where things get actually useful.

Because a decent model with strong workflows often beats a smarter model with chaos around it.

That’s the uncomfortable truth.

user.name: Amal Roy
user.description: Sharing Insights on AI, Tech, Free Tools & Free Resources. DM for collaboration/Paid promotion 📨 itsamalroy@gmail.com

tweets.full_text:
Most people use AI agents with:

«one giant system prompt
20 pasted instructions
random tools
hope it works»

That gets messy fast.

Awesome Claude Plugins points toward a cleaner model:

«capability composition.»

And honestly…

this is how agent systems start becoming actual software.

Not prompt experiments.

Because useful agents rarely need:

«one big brain.»

They need:

«specialized capabilities working together.»

Search.
Files.
Automation.
Retrieval.
External actions.
Domain-specific tools.

That’s a much better mental model.

Feels less like:

«chat with smarter AI»

and more like:

«assembling an execution stack.»

That’s where things get interesting.

Because I think most people still overestimate:

«model intelligence»

and underestimate:

«tool architecture.»

A mediocre model with excellent tools + workflows can outperform a smarter model trapped in a chat box.

That’s the uncomfortable truth.

Curated repos like this matter because the ecosystem is getting chaotic.

Every week:

«new plugin
new MCP server
new connector
new automation layer
new agent framework»

Signal gets buried fast.

Good curation becomes leverage.

user.name: Amal Roy
user.description: Sharing Insights on AI, Tech, Free Tools & Free Resources. DM for collaboration/Paid promotion 📨 itsamalroy@gmail.com

tweets.full_text:
Most people use AI agents like this:

«one giant prompt
dump context
hope it figures things out»

That works…

until workflows get messy.

Anthropic’s Claude Plugins repo hints at a much cleaner direction:

«modular capabilities.»

And honestly…

this is how serious agent systems probably get built.

Not with:

«bigger prompts.»

But with:

«composable tools.»

Because real work isn’t one skill.

An actual useful agent needs different capabilities for different jobs:

«search
retrieval
document handling
external actions
workflow execution
data access
specialized operations»

That’s a very different mental model from:

«chat with smarter AI.»

Feels more like software architecture.

Less like prompting.

That’s the interesting shift.

Most beginners still think better AI = better answers.

But in practice, useful agents often improve because:

«the tool layer got better.»

Not the model.

That’s a huge mindset change.

Because once capabilities become modular…

AI starts looking less like:

«one assistant»

and more like:

«an operating environment.»

And honestly…

that’s where things get much more interesting.

The moat may not be:

«best model.»

It may be:

«best tool ecosystem.»

user.name: Amal Roy
user.description: Sharing Insights on AI, Tech, Free Tools & Free Resources. DM for collaboration/Paid promotion 📨 itsamalroy@gmail.com

tweets.full_text:
Most people learn Claude Code like this:

«watch random demos
copy commands
break stuff
hope it clicks»

That’s a painful way to learn.

This repo is a much better approach.

Because instead of treating Claude Code like:

«magic AI coding»

…it breaks things down from scratch.

And honestly…

that’s exactly what most builders need.

The useful shift:

People keep focusing on:

«which coding agent is best»

But the bigger bottleneck is usually:

«operator skill.»

A powerful tool with weak workflows is still weak.

That’s the uncomfortable truth.

Claude Code from Scratch looks useful because it focuses on practical usage patterns instead of hype-driven “AI writes everything” nonsense.

What actually matters when using coding agents:

«context management
task decomposition
tool usage
review loops
debugging workflows
execution control
handoffs»

Not just prompting.

That’s where beginners get stuck.

Because AI coding is less:

«ask for code»

and more:

«supervise execution.»

Big difference.

Feels like the real skill in AI coding is becoming:

«being a good operator.»

Not just having access to better models.

And I think most people still underestimate that.

tweets.full_text:
Most people use AI agents like this:

«one giant prompt
hope for the best
retry when it fails»

That works…

until the tasks get real.

Anthropic’s Skills repo points at a much smarter idea:

«teach agents reusable operational skills.»

Not bigger prompts.

Not bigger context dumps.

Actual modular capabilities.

And honestly…

this is one of the cleaner mental models for agent design.

Because real work is repetitive.

You keep doing variations of:

«debugging
code review
documentation
refactoring
investigation
architecture analysis
deployment workflows»

So why keep re-explaining the same workflows every time?

That’s inefficient.

The useful shift:

AI agents may get better less from:

«raw intelligence gains»

and more from:

«structured operational knowledge.»

That’s a different game.

Feels a bit like giving agents:

«playbooks.»

Instead of just instructions.

That matters because good operators don’t improvise everything from scratch.

They use repeatable systems.

Same logic here. Anthropic’s repo provides example reusable “skills” for Claude workflows.

I think most builders are still underestimating this layer.

The real moat may not be:

«best model»

It may be:

«best workflows + best skills.»
